title: Design Notes
subtitle: Why spacing is more emotional than you think
paragraphs: Design isn't just about how something looks — it's about how it feels. And nothing affects feeling more quietly (and more dramatically) than spacing. You can show someone two layouts with identical typography and color — and they'll tell you one "feels calmer" or "feels sharper."

The more space you give a headline, the more important it feels. Tight layout says: "Here's everything right now." Wide layout says: "Pause. Look at this." White space isn't empty — it's loaded with mood.

Note 1: Padding creates tone

Not everything has to be perfectly symmetrical. In fact, small shifts — like slightly tighter spacing between a title and subtitle, or more margin under a paragraph than above — can make your design feel less robotic.

Note 2: Uneven beats feel human

Balanced ≠ equal. Sometimes the numbers lie. You can have two perfectly measured objects that look misaligned. Train your eye — and trust it more than your layout grid when it comes to visual weight. It's not cheating. It's good design.

Note 3: Don't trust rulers, trust your eye

These aren't rules. Just observations — from one layout to the next. Every project has its own rhythm, but spacing is always part of the music.
